questions.question:
Should the ticket standardize on REST plus a streaming channel, instead of a single WebSocket API for everything?

Why it matters: The implementation shape, test strategy, and renderer client abstraction depend on whether existing IPC request/response calls become HTTP endpoints or are modeled as bidirectional socket messages.
Recommended answer: Use REST endpoints for normal commands and queries, and add WebSocket or SSE only for live run/chat/progress events.

Should the local browser URL be intended only for trusted local development, or should the ticket include auth/origin hardening for broader access on the local network?

Why it matters: Exposing main-process project and filesystem operations over HTTP changes the security boundary; the ticket needs the right acceptance criteria for binding, origin checks, tokens, and project access guards.
Recommended answer: Treat it as trusted local development only for the first pass: bind to localhost, require a generated session token, and reuse existing registered-project/safe-ID guards.
questions.answer: Initially lcoal development, but later down the line I may release a cli with --web that runs a local server and opens the browser, but for now I just want to go to the url as a developer and see the UI working in both

questions.question:
Should this task migrate all renderer IPC usage in one pass, or create the transport layer and migrate only the app paths needed for board/ticket workflows first?

Why it matters: A full IPC removal may be larger than a task-sized implementation and risks becoming a broad rewrite; a vertical slice can make Chrome URL testing work sooner while preserving compatibility.
Recommended answer: Deliver a vertical slice: add the bridge/server/client abstraction and migrate core project, board, ticket detail, ticket creation/update, and run status paths, leaving low-risk remaining IPC cleanup for follow-up tickets.
